_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Lists / Arrays
summary: Keep several values in order with a vector.
refresher: `std::vector` is C++'s common growable collection. Indexes begin at zero, `.size()` gives the number of elements, and `.push_back(value)` adds one item to the end.

blocks.body: A vector keeps several values of the same type in order. Create one with `std::vector<int> scores{80, 90};`. The type inside angle brackets says this vector stores integers.

A vector can grow with `push_back`. Its positions begin at zero, just like positions in a string. Brackets read a position when you already know it is valid. `.at(index)` checks the position and reports an error when it is outside the vector.

blocks.title: A vector is close to a Python list
blocks.body: A Python list and a C++ vector both store ordered values and start at index zero. A vector normally keeps one element type, so `std::vector<int>` accepts integers rather than a mixture of unrelated values.

blocks.caption: The vector begins with two integers, grows to three, and then prints its second value and its size.
Ask for checked access while a boundary is uncertain.

blocks.body: Bracket access trusts that the index is valid. `.at()` checks first and produces a clear out-of-range error when the position does not exist. While learning or debugging, that message is often more useful than an unexplained crash or incorrect value.

blocks.title: The size is not a valid index
blocks.body: A vector with three elements has indexes zero, one, and two. Index three equals the size and is already past the end. Keep an index below `.size()`.

blocks.prompt: Which is the last valid index for size three?
blocks.why: Zero-based positions are zero, one, and two, so the count itself is outside the vector.
Vector provides push_back to add one element at its end.
